source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 17 Conclusions - shell tips

questions.q: What does the -- marker do in a command line?
options: Marks the end of options, so anything after it is treated as an argument even if it starts with a dash
Comments out the rest of the line
Runs the command in the background
questions.why: -- ends option parsing; e.g. 'mkdir -p -- --hard' creates a directory literally named --hard instead of reading --hard as options.

questions.q: Jump back to the PREVIOUS working directory.
questions.why: cd - returns to the last directory; cd with no argument goes to your home directory.

questions.q: The '[' used in an if test is actually what?
options: A real program (/usr/bin/[), like true and false
Special shell syntax with no backing binary
questions.why: [ is a genuine command (/usr/bin/[), equivalent to test; true and false are also real binaries.

questions.q: What does the key sequence Ctrl+X Ctrl+E do?
options: Opens the command you are typing in your editor ($EDITOR), running it when you close the file
questions.why: Ctrl+X Ctrl+E edits the current command line in $EDITOR - a lifesaver for long one-liners.

questions.q: Using brace expansion, copy file.conf to a backup named file.conf.bak in one short command.
questions.why: {,.bak} expands to two strings (empty, then .bak), so this becomes 'cp file.conf file.conf.bak'.

options: Re-runs the previous command prefixed with sudo
Escalates the shell to root permanently
questions.why: !! is the previous command, so 'sudo !!' reruns it with sudo - the reflex for when you forgot sudo. (!$ reuses just the last argument.)
